questions.scenario: A low-risk internal report can wait until tomorrow. The team keeps backups and a documented restore process, but no standby compute is running.

options.text: Backup and restore, with low steady cost and longer recovery time.
Active-active, because no compute is running in a second site.
Warm standby, because backups are the same as a full running stack.
Zone redundancy, because daily reports always require multi-region traffic serving.

questions.explanation: Backup and restore can be a good fit when the workflow can wait and low steady cost matters. The promise should still be documented and tested.

questions.scenario: The orders service keeps core data, identity, networking, and secrets ready, but most compute is scaled to zero until recovery. The team can start compute around the prepared foundation.

options.text: Pilot light, because the basics stay ready while compute is scaled up during recovery.
Active-active, because scaled-to-zero compute is serving live traffic.
No recovery strategy, because anything below active-active is invalid.
Blob soft delete, because soft delete starts app compute.

questions.explanation: Pilot light keeps the foundation ready and starts or scales compute during recovery. It costs more than pure restore but less than a full warm standby.

questions.scenario: A small full stack is already running in a standby region. During a regional incident, the team scales it up and moves traffic after smoke tests.

options.text: Warm standby, because a smaller working stack is already running and can be scaled up.
Backup and restore, because no runtime exists until after the restore.
Only tag-based cost allocation, because tags move traffic.
Blob versioning, because object versions route users between regions.

questions.explanation: Warm standby pays steady cost for a small complete stack so recovery is faster than starting from backups and empty compute.

options.text: Active-active can offer the fastest regional recovery, but it has high cost, data consistency, routing, and testing complexity.
Storage redundancy protects infrastructure failure better than object-level mistakes; bad writes may still need backup, version, or repair plans.
A restore drill should record actual restore time, data gap, manual steps, and surprises.
Once a strategy name is chosen, the team no longer needs smoke tests or observability.

questions.explanation: Recovery strategy is an operating promise, not just a label. It needs cost review, data protection, traffic behavior, testing, and evidence from drills.
